title: Suminagashi ink preparation
subtitle: Mixing sumi, carmine, and ox-gall ratios for clean concentric suminagashi rings
excerpt: Suminagashi marbling depends on the right balance of ink and ox-gall. Too much gall and the rings spread so wide they leave gaps; too little and the rings lock against each other rather than floating freely. This tutorial covers the preparation of two inks, the testing drop, and the balance adjustment sequence.
sourceNotes: Suminagashi is the oldest surviving marbling tradition, originating in Heian-period Japan. No single pre-1928 English-language source covers the ink preparation in technical detail. Synthesised from contemporary craft documentation and consistent with general principles in Halfer (1894) regarding surfactant behaviour in marbling.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Japanese ink-on-water marbling tradition, meaning 'floating ink'. Sumi and ox-gall are alternated to create concentric ring patterns on still water.
A surfactant derived from bile, added to marbling inks to reduce surface tension. Each drop of ox-gall-laden ink pushes surrounding ink outward as it is deposited.
The proportion of ox-gall to ink in a suminagashi ink mixture, which determines how far each ink drop spreads on the water surface.

content.text: Suminagashi works because of the competition between two inks. Sumi ink has no surfactant and floats on the water surface. The ox-gall-bearing ink has a surfactant that pushes surrounding ink outward on contact. Loading one brush with sumi and alternating with a second brush carrying ox-gall ink creates concentric rings as the two fluids push each other apart.
What you need
Sumi ink: pure sumi (carbon-black or pine-soot based), undiluted. The sumi brush carries no ox-gall.
Ox-gall working solution:
ox-gall
diluted 1:3 with distilled water. This is the second ink; its
gall-ratio
determines the ring spacing.
Two fine fude brushes: one dedicated to sumi, one dedicated to the ox-gall solution. Do not mix brushes.
Water: still, fresh tap water in the marbling tray. Room temperature, not cold.
The test drop sequence
Load the sumi brush lightly. Touch the tip to the water surface. The sumi should float in a small, defined dot about 10 to 20 mm in diameter.

Load the ox-gall brush lightly. Touch the tip to the centre of the sumi dot. The gall solution should push the sumi outward in a clean expanding ring. The ring should stop expanding when about 30 to 50 mm in diameter.
If the sumi dot breaks apart or the ring is ragged: the gall concentration is too high. Dilute the ox-gall solution with more distilled water.
If the sumi does not push outward when the gall ink is applied: the gall concentration is too low. Add a small amount of neat ox-gall to the working solution.
Drain and refill the tray with fresh water between testing sequences. The surfactant accumulates in the water and distorts subsequent tests.
Once the balance is set
With the correct
suminagashi
balance, the rings build one inside the other as the two brushes are alternated. The rings can be blown gently with the breath to introduce movement before printing.
Troubleshooting

attrs.symptom: Rings stop building after two or three pairs; the water surface seems inert
attrs.cause: Surfactant has built up in the water; the surface can no longer respond to new gall drops.

content.text: Drain and refill the tray with fresh water for each new print. Suminagashi requires completely fresh water for every sheet.
